Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings, but with everything going on in my life and limited resources, it rarely went as planned. I mostly studied when I could grab moments here and there, listening to lecture recordings when I had access to a computer at the library or community center. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I couldn't stick to regular times because I don't have internet at home, so I had to work around when I could access the LMS at public places. Deadlines definitely pushed me to find time somehow, even if it meant staying late somewhere with wifi.
Q3: Usually it was knowing an assignment was coming up or that I'd fallen behind on watching lectures. Sometimes my peer support group would message me reminding me about something, and that would push me to find a way to log in. The anxiety of not knowing what I was missing also drove me to check in when I could.
Q4: Not really a normal routine, no. Since I don't have internet at home, using the LMS was always an event I had to plan around. The closest thing to routine was going to the library on certain days when I knew it would be less crowded and I could actually get computer time.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded sessions were lifesavers for me since I learn best by listening. I'd download them when I could and listen later. The discussion boards were also helpful because I could see what other students were confused about and sometimes find answers without having to ask myself.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials and PDFs because downloading them took forever with slow connections, and I couldn't always access them later without internet. Some of the supplementary videos I just never got to because I had to prioritize what was actually graded.
Q7: If I could tell it would directly help me pass an assignment or understand something I was struggling with, it felt worth it. Also, anything I could download and use offline was valuable to me. Activities that required constant internet access felt less worthwhile because I couldn't always complete them.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford time-wise. With my situation, I had to be strategic about what I engaged with. Required activities got my attention because they affected my grade, but optional ones often got pushed aside even when I was curious about them.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio and listen on my phone during commutes or while doing chores. I borrowed textbooks from classmates and took handwritten notes. A lot of my learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or listening to downloaded lectures, and none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also meet with my peer support group in person to discuss concepts, which was really valuable learning but completely invisible online.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access materials once, download whatever I could, and then study them for days offline. One login might fuel a whole week of studying for me. The LMS would show I barely logged in, but I was actually working with those materials extensively.
Q12: There were definitely hard moments, especially when my anxiety spiked about falling behind. Not having reliable internet made everything harder. Sometimes I'd go a week or two without being able to access the LMS properly, and catching up felt overwhelming. The peer support helped pull me through those times.
Q13: All of those things affected me. Fatigue from juggling everything, confusion when I couldn't ask questions in real-time, and the constant stress of limited resources. I'd sometimes log in exhausted and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything because I was too tired to focus.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd log in and click through things just to feel like I was doing something, but my mind was elsewhere. When my anxiety was really high, I'd be on the LMS but not actually processing anything. It was like going through the motions without real engagement.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they required being online and thinking on the spot, which was hard with my access limitations. I knew they were important for participation, but the anxiety of having to respond quickly when I finally got online made me avoid them.
Q16: Deadlines controlled everything. I'd plan my trips to places with internet around when things were due. Sometimes I'd have to rush through assignments because I only had a small window of connectivity. It was stressful but it kept me from completely falling off track.
Q17: When I got feedback, it motivated me if it was encouraging, but sometimes it came too late for me to really use it for the next assignment. The peer support group was actually better for immediate feedback because we could discuss things in person without waiting.
Q18: The structure was challenging for someone in my situation. Having twelve online courses with lots of digital requirements assumed everyone had easy internet access. I appreciated when modules could be downloaded, but the interactive elements were hard to keep up with.
Q19: Downloadable content made engagement easier. What made it harder was anything requiring real-time participation or constant connectivity. The discussion requirements were tough because I couldn't always respond within the expected timeframes given my access issues.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in to download things or check what they need to do, but then doing the actual work offline and maybe struggling to submit it. Or someone who's anxious and checking constantly but too overwhelmed to actually complete the work.
Q21: That's basically my story. My LMS activity looks low because I don't have regular internet, but I was studying those downloaded materials extensively. I passed my exams because I actually learned the content, just not in ways the system could track.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and what you clicked on, but they miss all the offline learning, the group discussions, the time spent with physical notes. They can't measure understanding or effort, just digital footprints. For someone like me, they show maybe a quarter of my actual engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and putting in effort to understand it, not just going through motions. It's about being mentally present and curious, even when circumstances make it hard. For me, engagement often happened away from the computer entirely.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my peer support group meetings where we'd work through problems together for hours. It wouldn't show me listening to lectures on repeat during bus rides, or the anxiety I pushed through to keep going despite everything. It misses the human struggle and resilience behind the numbers.
